records.ety: [L. digestus, p. p. of digerere to separate, arrange, dissolve, digest; di- = dis- + gerere to bear, carry, wear. See Jest.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Digested; p. pr. & vb. n. Digesting.]

defs: To distribute or arrange methodically; to work over and classify; to reduce to portions for ready use or application; as, to digest the laws, etc.
quotes: Joining them together and digesting them into order.
We have cause to be glad that matters are so well digested.

defs: To separate (the food) in its passage through the alimentary canal into the nutritive and nonnutritive elements; to prepare, by the action of the digestive juices, for conversion into blood; to convert into chyme.
To think over and arrange methodically in the mind; to reduce to a plan or method; to receive in the mind and consider carefully; to get an understanding of; to comprehend.
quotes: Feelingly digest the words you speak in prayer.
How shall this bosom multiplied digest The senate's courtesy?

defs: To appropriate for strengthening and comfort.
quotes: Grant that we may in such wise hear them [the Scriptures], read, mark, learn, and inwardly digest them.

defs: Hence: To bear comfortably or patiently; to be reconciled to; to brook.
quotes: I never can digest the loss of most of Origin's works.

defs: To soften by heat and moisture; to expose to a gentle heat in a boiler or matrass, as a preparation for chemical operations.
To dispose to suppurate, or generate healthy pus, as an ulcer or wound.
To quiet or abate, as anger or grief.
To undergo digestion; as, food digests well or ill.
To suppurate; to generate pus, as an ulcer.

records.ety: [L. digestum, pl. digesta, neut., fr. digestus, p. p.: cf. F. digeste. See Digest, v. t.]

defs: That which is digested; especially, that which is worked over, classified, and arranged under proper heads or titles

A compilation of statutes or decisions analytically arranged. The term is applied in a general sense to the Pandects of Justinian (see Pandect), but is also specially given by authors to compilations of laws on particular topics; a summary of laws; as, Comyn's Digest; the United States Digest.
quotes: A complete digest of Hindu and Mahommedan laws after the model of Justinian's celebrated Pandects.
They made a sort of institute and digest of anarchy, called the Rights of Man.
